subject: Physics
Physics
Physics
solution:
From the source $v = V_0 \sin(300t)$, the angular frequency is $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$.
Capacitance $C = 100\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$, so $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.

Case 1 ($S_1$ closed, $S_2$ open):
Closing $S_1$ short-circuits $L_2$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_1$. With phase difference $\phi_1 = 30^\circ$:
$\tan 30^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R}$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow R = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C)$ ... (1)

Case 2 ($S_2$ closed, $S_1$ open):
Closing $S_2$ short-circuits $L_1$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_2$. With phase difference $\phi_2 = 60^\circ$:
$\tan 60^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R}$
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow \omega L_2 - X_C = R\sqrt{3}$ ... (2)

Substituting $R$ from (1) into (2):
$\omega L_2 - X_C = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C) \cdot \sqrt{3}$
$\omega L_2 - X_C = 3\omega L_1 - 3X_C$
$2X_C = 3\omega L_1 - \omega L_2 = \omega(3L_1 - L_2)$

Therefore:
$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2X_C}{\omega} = \dfrac{2}{\omega^2 C}$

Substituting $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$ and $C = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$:
$\omega^2 C = (300)^2 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^4 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9$

$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2}{9}\text{ H}$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ \dfrac{2}{9}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
In a pure inductive circuit current always lags behind the emf by $\frac{\pi}{2}$.
If $\mathrm{v}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{v}_0 \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$ then $I=I_0 \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ Now, given $v(t)=100 \sin (500 t)$ and $\mathrm{I}_0=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{\omega \mathrm{L}}=\frac{100}{500 \times 0.02}[\because \mathrm{L}=0.02 \mathrm{H}]$ $\mathrm{I}_0=10 \sin \left(500 \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ $\mathrm{I}_0=-10 \cos (500 \mathrm{t})$

⇒cos ϕ=80100⇒810

Thus, the value of x=8.

subject: Physics
solution:
At very high frequencies, inductor acts as an open circuit and capacitor as short circuit.

So, capacitive reactance, XC=1ωC≈0

Also, inductive reactance, XL=ωL≈∞

Thus, equivalent circuit can be redrawn as

Here, both 4 Ω resistances are in parallel and together they are in series with1 Ω and 2 Ω resistances.

Equivalent impedance is Z=1+4×44+4+2=5 Ω

Effective current is I=220 V5 Ω=44 A

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: Magnetic field energy $= 16$ J, Thermal power dissipated $= 32$ W, $I_{rms} = 2$ A, $f = 50$ Hz.
From energy stored in inductor:
$\frac{1}{2}L I_{rms}^2 = 16$
$\frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot 4 = 16$
$L = 8 \text{ H}$
From thermal power dissipated:
$I_{rms}^2 R = 32$
$4R = 32$
$R = 8 \; \Omega$
Inductive reactance:
$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 8 = 2512 \; \Omega$
$\frac{X_L}{R} = \frac{2512}{8} = 314$

$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(2 \pi \times 50 \times 1)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(100 \pi)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{V}{2}=\frac{100 \pi}{\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\max }=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \text { Ampere }
\end{aligned}$
Correct Answer : 1

solution:
The average rate at which energy supplied i.e. power delivered is maximum in case of the resonance, therefore

XC=XL

⇒1ωC=79.6 Ω

⇒   C=1ω×79.6=12πf×79.6=1100π×79.6=40  μF

Hence, C=40 μF.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let current in the inductance is iL, through the capacitor is iC and through resistance is iR at any instance as shown in the figure.

According to the Kirchoff's junction law, iR=iL+iC.

At resonance XC=XL hence iL=iC. Also, current through the capacitor and inductor have phase difference of π rad. Therefore, they will form destructive interference and hence the current through the resistance will be 0.

AC generator-- Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. A→II.

Galvanometer-- Detects the presence of current in the circuit B-I.

Transformer-- Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value C-IV.

Metal detector-- Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit D-III.
